title: Collapse during basketball in a young athlete

chart.oneLiner: 19M collegiate athlete presents after collapsing during a basketball game; he reports prior exertional lightheadedness, palpitations, and a family history of sudden death.

opening: I blacked out in the middle of a game today. I've felt dizzy and like my heart was pounding when I sprint before, but this time I just dropped.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 19-year-old male collegiate basketball player, fit and athletic, now calm but worried. Good historian.

HPI: Syncope occurred DURING peak exertion (sprinting down the court) — a high-risk feature for cardiac syncope. Sudden collapse with rapid spontaneous recovery, NO postictal confusion, NO tongue-biting, NO incontinence. Over the past several months: exertional lightheadedness/presyncope and palpitations with sprinting, plus occasional exertional chest tightness and breathlessness that improve with rest. Symptoms are WORSE with standing up quickly and after a big meal or dehydration. NO prodrome of warmth/nausea/sweating to suggest a simple faint. NO seizure aura.
FAMILY HISTORY: father died suddenly at age 41 (collapsed, no warning); a paternal uncle had an implanted defibrillator and 'a thick heart' (only if asked about family history of heart disease or sudden death).
PMH: none. No prior cardiac evaluation. MEDS: none; no performance/stimulant supplements (if asked). SOCIAL: no tobacco, no alcohol, no recreational drugs/cocaine; competitive athlete.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: well-appearing, fit young man at rest.
- Vitals: BP 126/78, HR 72, RR 16, 36.9°C, SpO2 99% RA.
- Cardiac: harsh CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO systolic murmur best heard at the LEFT LOWER STERNAL BORDER/apex; the murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing and DECREASES with squatting/handgrip; does NOT radiate to the carotids; sustained/forceful apical impulse (sometimes double). Carotid upstroke is BRISK/bifid (not delayed).
- Lungs: clear. Neuro: no focal deficit; no tongue laceration.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Emphasize the EXERTIONAL onset of the syncope only if asked when/during what it happened.
- Reveal the family history of sudden death / 'thick heart' / defibrillator ONLY if asked about family history of heart disease, cardiomyopathy, or sudden/early death.
- The murmur, its dynamic change with Valsalva/standing/squatting, the lack of carotid radiation, the brisk carotid upstroke, and the apical impulse are revealed only if the heart/carotids are examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.BMP: Within normal limits (no electrolyte derangement to explain arrhythmia or syncope).
labs.Echo: Echocardiogram: asymmetric septal hypertrophy (septum ~19 mm) with systolic anterior motion (SAM) of the mitral valve and a dynamic left ventricular outflow tract gradient (>30 mmHg at rest, rising with provocation); preserved/hyperdynamic EF — diagnostic of hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy.
labs.Holter: Ambulatory monitor: nonsustained ventricular tachycardia runs — a marker of arrhythmic/sudden-death risk.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep, narrow ('dagger-like') septal Q waves in the lateral/inferior leads and repolarization (strain) abnormalities; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy' library page — download an example tracing that matches: marked LVH by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization changes; sinus rhythm; no acute STEMI — and self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing marked LVH by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and strain-pattern repolarization changes in a young patient.
searchTerms: LVH dagger Q waves septal hypertrophy EKG
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 19-year-old man collapses during sports and reports exertional breathlessness and palpitations.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process (cardiomegaly is often absent because hypertrophy is concentric/intramural).
cxr.verify: Pick a CC PA chest film that is normal; nothing acute. Self-host with appropriate credit.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph that is essentially normal with a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs.
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 21-year-old athlete with exertional syncope and a murmur undergoes a chest film to assess the heart.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential for this exertional syncope (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy causing exertional syncope (leading & CANT-MISS,9). Ventricular arrhythmia / inherited channelopathy (long QT, etc.) (CANT-MISS,4). Anomalous coronary artery / acute ischemia (CANT-MISS,2). Aortic stenosis (core,3). Arrhythmogenic / other structural disease (core,2). Connects exertional syncope + dynamic murmur that LOUDENS with Valsalva + family history of sudden death to dynamic LVOT obstruction (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy causing exertional syncope. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Ventricular arrhythmia / inherited channelopathy (e.g., long QT); Anomalous coronary artery / acute ischemia. Also reasonable: Aortic stenosis; Arrhythmogenic/other structural cardiomyopathy.

criticalActions.item: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy causing exertional syncope

coreActions.item: Connects exertional syncope + a dynamic murmur that loudens with Valsalva + family history of sudden death to dynamic LVOT obstruction

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: 12-lead EKG (CANT-MISS,4). Transthoracic echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test (CANT-MISS,6). Ambulatory/Holter monitor and telemetry for arrhythmia (2). Troponin (1). BMP/electrolytes (1). CXR (1). PENALTY: clearing the athlete to return to play / discharging exertional syncope with a dynamic murmur before an echo (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Troponin; BMP; Glucose; Echo; Holter.

criticalActions.item: Transthoracic echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test

penalties.item: clearing the athlete to return to play / discharging exertional syncope with a dynamic murmur before an echo

requiredTests: Transthoracic echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test
contraindicatedTests: clearing the athlete to return to play / discharging exertional syncope with a dynamic murmur before an echo
orderSensitiveActions: clearing the athlete to return to play / discharging exertional syncope with a dynamic murmur before an echo

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = marked LVH by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and strain-pattern repolarization changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (4); identifies LVH by voltage with abnormal Q waves/repolarization changes (6); notes no acute STEMI and checks the QT (3); systematic read (2). PENALTY: calling the study normal or over-calling acute ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: identifies LVH by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization changes
notes no acute STEMI and assesses the QT interval
criticalFindings: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
commonMistakes: calling the EKG normal in a young athlete and attributing the LVH solely to athletic conditioning
steps.idealAnswer: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.

coreActions.item: identifies LVH by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization changes
systematic read, notes no acute STEMI and assesses the QT

penalties.item: calling the study normal / over-calling acute ischemia

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal/near-normal film (5); excludes acute pulmonary pathology and a widened mediastinum (3); notes that a normal heart size does NOT exclude HCM (2). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: identifies a normal or near-normal cardiac silhouette
notes a normal heart size does not exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
criticalFindings: Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process.
commonMistakes: expecting an enlarged heart and calling the study abnormal, or assuming a normal film excludes structural heart disease
steps.idealAnswer: Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process — a normal film does not exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process.

coreActions.item: identifies a normal or near-normal cardiac silhouette
notes a normal heart size does not exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The echo shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy with systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve and a dynamic LVOT gradient, and the Holter captured nonsustained VT. What is your diagnosis and what did you rule out?
steps.rubric: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy (6). Cites asymmetric septal hypertrophy + SAM + dynamic LVOT obstruction on echo, distinguishes the dynamic murmur from fixed aortic stenosis (loudens with Valsalva, no carotid radiation, brisk upstroke), and recognizes the family history of sudden death plus NSVT as markers of arrhythmic/sudden-death risk (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy. The echo shows asymmetric septal hypertrophy, systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, and a dynamic LVOT gradient — distinguishing it from fixed aortic stenosis (the murmur loudens with Valsalva, does not radiate to the carotids, and the carotid upstroke is brisk). I ruled out fixed valvular AS and a primary channelopathy; the family history of sudden death plus the NSVT on Holter mark a high arrhythmic/sudden-death risk.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH cardiomyopathies/HCM: restrict from competitive sports and strenuous exertion (CANT-MISS,6); start a non-vasodilating beta-blocker (or verapamil if intolerant) for symptomatic LVOT obstruction (5); cardiology/electrophysiology referral with risk stratification for an ICD given syncope + family history of sudden death + NSVT (5); maintain volume/preload and avoid dehydration (3); first-degree relative screening and genetic counseling (3); avoid afterload/preload reducers — nitrates, diuretics, dihydropyridine CCBs (3). PENALTY: vasodilators/nitrates/aggressive diuresis worsening dynamic obstruction (-3); clearing the patient for return to competitive play (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Restrict from competitive athletics and strenuous exertion. Start a non-vasodilating beta-blocker (e.g., metoprolol) — verapamil if beta-blocker intolerant — to reduce dynamic LVOT obstruction and symptoms. Refer to cardiology/electrophysiology for sudden-death risk stratification; given unexplained exertional syncope, a strong family history of sudden death, and NSVT, an ICD is indicated for secondary/primary prevention. Maintain adequate hydration and preload; avoid dehydration. Avoid preload/afterload reducers — nitrates, diuretics, and dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers — which worsen obstruction. Screen first-degree relatives with ECG/echo and offer genetic counseling. Consider septal reduction (myectomy/alcohol ablation) for refractory symptoms.

criticalActions.item: Restrict from competitive sports / strenuous exertion

coreActions.item: Cardiology/EP referral and ICD risk stratification (syncope + family history + NSVT)
First-degree relative screening and genetic counseling

bonusActions.item: Consider septal reduction therapy (myectomy/alcohol ablation) if refractory

requiredActions: Restrict from competitive sports / strenuous exertion
optionalActions: Cardiology/EP referral and ICD risk stratification (syncope + family history + NSVT)
First-degree relative screening and genetic counseling
Consider septal reduction therapy (myectomy/alcohol ablation) if refractory
steps.disposition: Admit; restrict exertion and expedite risk stratification

standardizedPatient.communicationStyle: answers directly but does not volunteer extra information
standardizedPatient.affect: 19-year-old male collegiate athlete, fit, now calm but worried. Good historian.
disclosureRules: Do not reveal sensitive history unless directly asked
Do not mention or hint at the diagnosis
Do not volunteer hidden exam or diagnostic findings
Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing

triggerConcepts: anyone die suddenly or young in the family
relatives with cardiomyopathy or a defibrillator
historyTriggers.response: My dad died suddenly when he was 41 — he just collapsed, no warning. And my uncle on my dad's side has a thick heart and got some kind of device put in to shock it.

triggerConcepts: what were you doing when it happened
historyTriggers.response: It happened right in the middle of the game while I was sprinting down the court — not while I was resting.

triggerConcepts: any warning before you passed out
felt warm or nauseous or sweaty first
historyTriggers.response: No real warning — I didn't feel warm, sick, or sweaty first. I came right back to myself quickly and wasn't confused afterward.
When I sprint I sometimes feel my heart pounding and get a little chest tightness and short of breath, but it eases off when I stop and rest.
No, I didn't bite my tongue, there was no shaking or jerking, and I didn't wet myself.
It feels worse when I stand up fast, after a big meal, or when I haven't had enough to drink.
No, I don't use any drugs, cocaine, or stimulant supplements, and I don't smoke or drink.
I've never fully passed out before, but I've gotten lightheaded a few times when sprinting over the last few months.
I've always been healthy — no medical problems and I've never had my heart checked out before.

The systolic murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing and DECREASES with squatting/handgrip; it does NOT radiate to the carotids.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: No change in the murmur with maneuvers and no radiation appreciated.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Carotid upstroke is BRISK/bifid (not delayed); no carotid bruit and no radiated murmur.
Sustained/forceful apical impulse, sometimes double; no displacement.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 19M collegiate athlete presents after collapsing during a basketball game; he reports prior exertional lightheadedness, palpitations, and a family history of sudden death. The diagnosis is Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam (a dynamic systolic murmur that loudens with Valsalva and does not radiate to the carotids), and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyHistoryClues: Syncope during peak exertion with no prodrome and rapid recovery (high-risk cardiac syncope).
Family history of sudden death at a young age and a relative with a 'thick heart' and a defibrillator.
Exertional palpitations, chest tightness, and presyncope worse with standing/dehydration.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: harsh CRESCENDO-DECRESCENDO systolic murmur best heard at the LEFT LOWER STERNAL BORDER/apex; the murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing and DECREASES with squatting/handgrip; does NOT radiate to the carotids; sustained/forceful apical impulse.
The systolic murmur INCREASES with Valsalva and standing and DECREASES with squatting/handgrip; it does NOT radiate to the carotids.
Carotid upstroke is BRISK/bifid (not delayed); no carotid bruit and no radiated murmur.
Sustained/forceful apical impulse, sometimes double; no displacement.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including the family history of sudden death only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal the dynamic LVOT-obstruction murmur and brisk carotid upstroke.
Build a differential led by Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes (ventricular arrhythmia/channelopathy, anomalous coronary/ischemia).
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (EKG, echo, Holter) and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy and manage with exertion restriction, beta-blockade, EP/ICD risk stratification, family screening, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Mistaking the dynamic murmur for fixed aortic stenosis or clearing a young athlete to return to play
managementPearls: Restrict from competitive sports / strenuous exertion
Non-vasodilating beta-blocker first-line; refer for ICD risk stratification given syncope, family history of sudden death, and NSVT
criticalWorkupItems: Transthoracic echocardiogram — the key diagnostic test
criticalManagementItems: Restrict from competitive sports / strenuous exertion
criticalInterpretationItems: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process.
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process.
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Marked left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage with deep narrow septal Q waves and repolarization (strain) changes; sinus rhythm; no acute ST elevation.
Essentially normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs; no acute process.
imageInterpretationPearls: A normal chest film does not exclude hypertrophic cardiomyopathy — the hypertrophy is intramural and the silhouette is often normal.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
